meta.title: From 5 days to 1 hour: migrating to Infrastructure as Code
meta.description: How Terraform + Cloud Init transformed our provisioning and the lessons learned migrating from manual infrastructure to Infrastructure as Code.
meta.ogTitle: From 5 days to 1 hour: migrating to Infrastructure as Code
meta.ogDescription: How Terraform + Cloud Init transformed our provisioning and the lessons learned.

content:
When I joined Colmena Tech, provisioning a server took 5 days. The process was a mix of tickets to the Cloud provider, manual SSH, scripts scattered across each developer's machine, and documentation that — when it existed — was already outdated. This article covers how we migrated to Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with Terraform and Cloud Init, reducing provisioning time to less than 1 hour.

The previous state: manual chaos

Before IaC, the provisioning cycle was:

  1. A developer requested a server via Slack
  2. Someone on the team accessed the Cloud Provider panel and created a VM
  3. SSH to the VM and manual installation of dependencies
  4. Firewall, user, and environment variable configuration
  5. Documentation in a wiki that was rarely updated

The result: server drift (never two alike), outages due to incorrect configurations, and a process no one wanted to do because something always went wrong.

Pro tip: If your team avoids deploying or provisioning infrastructure, that's a red flag. Automation is not a luxury, it's an operational necessity.

Step 1: Terraform for the base infrastructure

Terraform allowed us to describe all infrastructure as declarative code. We chose a monorepo approach with reusable modules.

# Estructura del repo
infrastructure/
├── environments/
│   ├── production/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── terraform.tfvars
│   └── staging/
│       ├── main.tf
│       └── terraform.tfvars
├── modules/
│   ├── compute/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   └── network/
│       ├── main.tf
│       └── variables.tf
└── providers.tf

The compute module ended up looking like this:

# modules/compute/main.tf
resource "oci_core_instance" "this" {
  compartment_id      = var.compartment_id
  availability_domain = var.availability_domain
  shape               = var.instance_shape
  display_name        = var.instance_name

  source_details {
    source_id   = var.image_id
    source_type = "image"
  }

  create_vnic_details {
    subnet_id        = var.subnet_id
    assign_public_ip = var.assign_public_ip
  }

  metadata = {
    ssh_authorized_keys = var.ssh_public_key
    user_data           = base64encode(var.cloud_init_template)
  }

  freeform_tags = var.tags
}

Step 2: Cloud Init for initial configuration

Cloud Init (cloud-config) allowed us to standardize the initial configuration of each server. Instead of an improvised bash script, we used a version-controlled YAML file.

# cloud-init.yaml
#cloud-config
package_update: true
package_upgrade: true

packages:
  - docker.io
  - docker-compose-v2
  - prometheus-node-exporter
  - fail2ban
  - htop

users:
  - name: deploy
    groups: sudo, docker
    shell: /bin/bash
    sudo: ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL
    ssh_authorized_keys:
      - ${ssh_key_deploy}

runcmd:
  - systemctl enable docker
  - systemctl start docker
  - ufw allow OpenSSH
  - ufw allow 80/tcp
  - ufw allow 443/tcp
  - ufw --force enable
  - echo "cloud-init completed" > /var/log/cloud-init-completed.log

The key was to template the cloud-init with environment-specific variables (SSH keys, hostname, etc.) using Terraform's templatefile().

# modules/compute/main.tf
data "template_file" "cloud_init" {
  template = file("${path.module}/cloud-init.yaml")
  vars = {
    ssh_key_deploy = var.ssh_public_key_deploy
    hostname       = var.instance_name
  }
}

Step 3: Remote state and collaboration

We moved from local state to Terraform Cloud with remote state. This enabled:

  • Team collaboration without overwriting state
  • Change history and runs
  • Automatic plans on Pull Requests (VCS-driven workflow)
  • State locking to prevent corruption
# providers.tf
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "colmenatech"
    workspaces {
      name = "infrastructure-production"
    }
  }
}

Lessons learned

1. Don't abstract too early

We started with very generic modules that tried to cover all possible cases. We ended up with complex, hard-to-read configurations. The lesson: start with flat code and extract modules when you see the pattern repeat at least 3 times.

2. Version the state, but protect it

Terraform state contains sensitive information. Use a remote backend with encryption at rest and access control. Never upload terraform.tfstate to the repository.

3. Cloud Init is not enough for everything

Cloud Init works for initial bootstrap, but ongoing server management requires additional tools. For post-deployment configuration we used Ansible and for containers, Docker Compose with watchtower for automatic updates.

4. Test plans in CI

We set up GitHub Actions to run terraform plan on every Pull Request. This caught syntax errors, unintended changes, and missing dependencies before reaching production.

# .github/workflows/terraform-plan.yml
name: Terraform Plan
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform init
      - run: terraform plan -no-color

5. Document the "why", not the "how"

Terraform code already describes the what and the how. Useful documentation explains why certain decisions were made: why OCI was chosen over AWS, why the VM.Standard.E4.Flex shape is used instead of ARM, etc.

Results

After 3 months of progressive migration:

  • Provisioning: from 5 days to 45 minutes
  • Drift: eliminated — every server is identical
  • Human errors: 90% reduction in incorrect configurations
  • Onboarding: new team members can provision an environment in minutes
  • Cloud bill: 25% reduction by eliminating orphan resources

Key metric: The time between "I need a server" and "the server is ready" went from 40 man-hours to 15 minutes fully automated.

Conclusion

Migrating to IaC is not just about tools. It's a cultural shift that requires discipline in code review, careful modularization, and context documentation. Terraform + Cloud Init were the starting point, but the journey continues with GitOps, Kubernetes, and platform automation.

If your team still provisions servers manually, start with a single service. Automate its complete creation with Terraform and Cloud Init. Once you see it working, scale to the rest. The first week of investment pays for itself many times over in the first month.
